2026-06-19T09:27:15.4494330Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4495092Z [189] ◐ Let me analyze this PR carefully from a supply-chain security perspective. I need to review the code snippets provided and produce a JSON response matching the panelist-return-schema.json format.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4495862Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4496340Z   Let me first load the relevant security documents and schema to ensure my review is grounded in the actual codebase contracts.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4496871Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4497148Z   Let me think through each security concern systematically:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4497480Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4497712Z   ## 1. shell=True + user-controlled cmd string
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4498024Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4498592Z   The `_execute_command` function uses `subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True, ...)` where `cmd` comes from `hook.effective_command` (either `hook.bash` or `hook.command`). 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4499230Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4499863Z   Key question: Who controls hook files? If hooks are defined in project's `.apm/hooks.json` committed to a public repo, then any machine that installs the project will execute those hooks. 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4500740Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4502900Z   This is essentially post-install code execution - one of the 8 threat model items APM must defend against. The `shell=True` with a user-controlled string creates a code injection surface if a malicious package could inject hooks into the project's `.apm/hooks.json` or if there's no explicit user consent before running hooks. However, if hooks are only in the project root `.apm/hooks.json` controlled by the project owner rather than packages, the threat model shifts to the project owner running commands on their own machine - which aligns with how npm handles lifecycle scripts during install, update, and uninstall operations.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4505558Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4507024Z   The `shell=True` parameter compounds the risk significantly: even if a hook command is supposed to invoke a specific binary, shell interpretation allows injection attacks if any part of the command string comes from package data like the package name, version, or path. This is a blocking severity issue since it represents the core post-install code execution threat.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4508402Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4509501Z   I'm also looking at environment variable expansion in HTTP headers - there's a function that replaces any environment variables found in header values, which could expose sensitive credentials if headers are ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4704 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4510452Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4510935Z [190] ✓ report_intent(intent: Reviewing PR supply-chain security)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4511223Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4512154Z [191] ✓ $ find /home/runner/work/apm/apm/.agents -name "*.json" 2>/dev/null | head -20 && find /home/runner/work/apm/apm -path "*/apm-review-panel*" -name "*.json" 2>/dev/null | head -10
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4512849Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4513451Z [192] ✓ $ find /home/runner/work/apm/apm -name "hook_executors.py" -o -name "hooks.py" 2>/dev/null | head -10
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4513843Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4514619Z [193] ✓ $ cat /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md 2>/dev/null | head -120
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4515114Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4515627Z [194] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR. Let me analyze each finding:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4516206Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4517816Z   1. **`[i]` in Rich Panel title (hooks.py:198)** — The `title="[i] Getting Started"` is using Rich markup notation. In Rich, `[i]` is an italic markup tag, not the literal string `[i]`. This means the Panel title will render as italic-formatted "Getting Started" text, not as `[i] Getting Started`. This is a real bug — the `[i]` symbol won't appear visually.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4519542Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4520998Z      The STATUS_SYMBOLS table shows `"info": "[i]"` — but since Rich interprets `[i]` as markup, passing it as a Panel title will silently strip `[i]` as an italic tag (or produce unexpected behavior). The fix is to escape the brackets: `title="\\[i] Getting Started"` or use `"[\\i] Getting Started"`.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4522288Z      
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4523437Z      To properly display the literal `[i]` symbol, I need to escape it with a backslash so Rich doesn't interpret it as a markup tag. The escaping approach `\\[i]` would render the intended `[i] Getting Started` text correctly.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4524691Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4526524Z      Now looking at the second issue with the HTTP hook — the `verbose_detail` message claims an event was "sent" immediately after the thread starts, but the actual completion happens asynchronously. This is misleading because the message fires before the thread finishes executing, so the event hasn't truly been sent yet when the log statement appears.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4528593Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4531066Z      There's also a stylistic inconsistency here: the `[i]` symbol is hardcoded directly into the message string rather than using the `STATUS_SYMBOLS` lookup like other parts of the code do. Since `verbose_detail` doesn't support a `symbol=` parameter, the code resorts to manually prefixing the symbol instead of using the standard approach. Still analyzing the implementation...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4532372Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4533096Z [195] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/ut..., view_range: [80, 175])
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4533529Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4534712Z [196] ✓ $ cd /home/runner/work/apm/apm && grep -n "user.*@\|userinfo\|netloc\|password" src/apm_cli/commands/hooks.py src/apm_cli/core/hook_executors.py src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py 2>/dev/null
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4535630Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4542792Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4548796Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4549635Z [202] ◐ Now I have a thorough picture. Let me check the init scaffold template closely and the `apm hooks` command behavior to understand the growth angle.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4550556Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4552730Z   Key observations:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4554027Z   1. The `apm hooks init` scaffold creates an echo command as the default - `echo 'Installed:' && cat` - this is an interesting choice but not very intuitive for a first-time user. A more compelling default would showcase the analytics angle.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4555640Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4557299Z   2. The doc is in `enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md` - buried in the enterprise section. This is a significant growth concern. Most developers don't look at enterprise docs. The feature has broad applicability (Slack notifications, CI notifications, personal automation) but the doc placement signals it's enterprise-only.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4559378Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4559629Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4561802Z   3. The README mentions hooks in a yaml example comment but doesn't surface `apm hooks` as a CLI command, which hurts discoverability. The CLI itself does provide good inline guidance when you run `apm hooks` with nothing set up, but there's no CHANGELOG entry for this significant feature addition. The npm lifecycle hooks parallel is compelling but the docs don't explicitly draw that connection.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4564088Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4565983Z   4. The init scaffold outputs the JSON payload which helps understand the structure, but it's not a practical first example - something like a Slack notification or file logging would be more useful for getting started. The `apm hooks test` command is a nice DevX touch for dry-running without triggering real installs.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4567817Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4569465Z   5. There's no community hook registry or published examples, which is a missed opportunity since npm's scripts are commonly shared. The docs also don't establish the mental model connection to npm hooks, which would help JS developers quickly grasp the concept.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4571221Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4572610Z   Now I'm checking the CHANGELOG to see if there's an Unreleased section where this feature should be documented. 2026-06-19T09:27:15.4602222Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4602540Z [217] ◐ Key finding! In `_rich_echo`, line 130:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4602879Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4603208Z   console.print(message, style=style_str, highlight=False, markup=False)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4603719Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4604002Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4605087Z   Note `markup=False`! This means Rich markup is DISABLED in `_rich_echo`. So `[i]` in the message string is safe — it will render as the literal string `[i]`.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4605819Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4606298Z   But in `hooks.py:191-200`, the Panel is constructed directly:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4606811Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4607200Z   console.print(
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4607795Z       Panel(
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4608219Z           "[bold]Next steps:[/bold]\n\n"
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4608818Z           f"  1. Edit [cyan]{target_file.relative_to(Path.cwd())}[/cyan] "
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4609323Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4609684Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4610010Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4610375Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4610755Z   Building the Panel with instructions...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4611217Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4612509Z   The key difference is that this `console.print(Panel(...))` call bypasses `_rich_echo` entirely, so the `markup=False` protection doesn't apply. Rich's default behavior is to parse markup, which means `[i]` in the Panel title will actually be interpreted as italic formatting rather than rendered as literal text.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4613686Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4614750Z   I need to trace where this `console` variable comes from in hooks.py to understand what instance is being used and whether it has any custom configuration that might affect markup handling.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4615491Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4616050Z [218] ✓ $ grep -n "^console\s*=\|^console:" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/utils/console.py

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4694540Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4696202Z [248] ◐ It seems like `console` is not actually exported at the module level from `console.py`. The `from apm_cli.utils.console import console` in hooks.py would fail with an ImportError since there's no module-level `console` variable in `console.py`. But this is caught by the `except (ImportError, NameError)` blocks at lines 91 and 202, so it would silently fall back to the plaintext output path.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4697622Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4698145Z   Wait, actually let me reconsider. The imports in hooks.py are:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4699060Z   1. Line 74: `from apm_cli.utils.console import console` — used in `hooks()` (list command) to print the table
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4699674Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4700056Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4700786Z   2. Line 189: `from apm_cli.utils.console import console` — used in `hooks_init()` to print the Panel
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4701357Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4702718Z   Both are inside try/except blocks that catch ImportError and NameError, so if the import fails, it falls back to click.echo. The real question is whether `console` is actually exported from console.py at the module level, or if it's only created inside the `_get_console()` function without being assigned to a module-level variable.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4703827Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4704435Z [249] ✓ $ tail -50 /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/utils/console.py

Let me check the test_service.py more carefully and look for whether the hook firing through service is tested.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4708578Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4709241Z [252] ✓ $ grep -n "fire\|hook\|lifecycle\|build_runner" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/tests/unit/install/test_service.py
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4709658Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4710500Z [253] ✓ $ grep -rn "_fire_update_hooks\|_fire_uninstall_hooks\|build_runner_from_context\|fire.*pre.update\|fire.*post.update\|fire.*pre.uninstall\|fire.*post.uninstall" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/tests --include="*.py" | head -20
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4711246Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4711652Z [254] ◐ Now I have all the information I need to generate the documentation review. Let me analyze the key findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4712199Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4712861Z   1. **"fire-and-forget" language accuracy**: The doc says on line 10: "Hooks are fire-and-forget: a failing hook never blocks the CLI." But this is actually **only partially true**:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4714484Z      - **HTTP hooks**: Truly fire-and-forget — they run in a daemon thread (`threading.Thread(target=_send, daemon=True)`) and do NOT block the CLI. ✓
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4715753Z      - **Command hooks**: **Synchronous** via `subprocess.run()` — they DO block the CLI for up to 30 seconds (or whatever `timeoutSec` is set to). 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4716452Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4717843Z   This inaccuracy appears in multiple places: the module docstring in lifecycle_hooks.py claims "All hooks are fire-and-forget," and the security considerations section repeats this claim with configurable timeouts. But command hooks are synchronous and definitely block execution, so these statements are misleading for that hook type.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4719322Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4722277Z   2. **Windows/cross-platform `command` vs `bash` distinction**: The doc needs to clarify the platform-specific behavior around command hooks. not explicit enough for Windows users. Policy hooks can't be disabled according to the docs, and that checks out with the code. The `commands.md` file is definitely missing an `apm hooks` section — that's a real gap. The security section has some issues too: it mentions "fire-and-forget" for commands which doesn't match the actual behavior, and while it's correct that verbose mode logs hook failures, the claim that they never block the CLI only applies to HTTP hooks, not local ones. Let me look at the sidebar ordering next.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4724637Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4726287Z   I'm checking the enterprise docs structure to see how this policy hooks doc should be ordered alongside the other governance pages. I can see governance-overview.md is set to order 1, but I need to look at what other enterprise docs exist and their ordering to figure out where this should fit. I'm also noticing the doc only covers the Unix policy directory path but Windows uses a different location en... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4601 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4745548Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4746002Z [267] ✓ $ grep -n "url" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/hook_executors.py | grep -i "log\|append\|target" | head -10
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4746462Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4746738Z [268] ◐ Now I have a complete picture. Let me analyze all the findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4747286Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4747918Z   1. **`requests` IS a declared dependency** - confirmed in pyproject.toml and uv.lock. So no finding there.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4748563Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4749743Z   2. **`shell=True` with user-controlled command string** - This is the post-install code execution threat. The command comes from `hook.effective_command` which is `hook.bash or hook.command` - both from the hook JSON file. The hook JSON files come from:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4750777Z      - `/etc/apm/policy.d/*.json` (admin-owned)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4751146Z      - `~/.apm/hooks/*.json` (user-owned)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4751479Z      - `.apm/hooks.json` (project-level)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4751940Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4753870Z   The project-level hooks file is where the real concern lies. If someone commits `.apm/hooks.json` to a public repository and another developer installs that repo as a dependency, the hooks would execute during their installation. However, looking at how `discover_hooks()` works, it loads from the current project's root directory using `Path.cwd()`, not from the installed package's directory, so hooks only run from the installing project itself, not from dependencies.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4755985Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4758559Z   But I need to verify this by tracing through the actual install command to see whether `project_root` gets set to the installed package's location or stays as the current working directory. Also, there's a significant security issue with `_expand_env_vars` - a hook author can inject environment variables like `$GITHUB_TOKEN` into headers and exfiltrate them to external URLs. For project-level hooks, this means the project owner could do this, which is less of a supply chain risk but still a social engineering concern if developers blindly copy committed hook files. For user-level hooks, it's the user's own tokens at risk, and for policy hooks there are different implications.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4761301Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4762765Z   The real danger emerges if this framework gets used for project hooks that are committed to repositories and shared - it becomes a standard attack vector where developers unknowingly run hooks that steal their authentication tokens. There's also the issue of credentials persisting in the... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 6025 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4764056Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4765301Z [269] ✓ $ grep -n "shell=True\|_build_hook_env\|_expand_env_vars\|target=\|url\[:30\]\|allow_redirects\|C:\\\\ProgramData\|policy.d\|requests\b" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/hook_executors.py | head -40
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4766062Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4766520Z [270] ✓ $ grep -n "url\[:30\]\|https://\|URL must" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/commands/hooks.py | head -20
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4766981Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4767539Z [271] ✓ $ grep -n "install\|hook\|fire\|runner" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/commands/install.py 2>/dev/null | grep -i "hook" | head -20
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4768084Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4768666Z [272] ✓ $ grep -rn "hook\|lifecycle\|fire_hook\|build_runner" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/commands/install.py 2>/dev/null | head -30
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4769196Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4769749Z [273] ✓ $ grep -rn "build_runner_from_context\|discover_hooks\|LifecycleHookRunner\|fire(" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli -l 2>/dev/null
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4770305Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4770877Z [274] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR. Let me analyze the CLI UX from a developer experience perspective and formulate my findings.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4771527Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4771921Z   Key UX issues I've identified:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4772649Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4773078Z   ## Required (blocking)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4773600Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4776163Z   1. **Windows scaffold template uses `bash` field** - The `_INIT_TEMPLATE` in hooks.py line 33 generates a starter with `"bash": "echo 'Installed:' && cat"`. This is Unix-only. On Windows, this command will fail silently (since hook failures are swallowed). A Windows user following `apm hooks init` → `apm hooks test` → gets no error feedback, just a mysteriously empty log. The template should include both `bash` and `command` fields, or at minimum call out the Windows incompatibility.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4777933Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4778223Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4780479Z   2. **`apm hooks test` success message is misleading for HTTP hooks** - The success message `"'{event}' event fired. Check ~/.apm/logs/hooks.log for output."` prints after `runner.fire()` returns, but HTTP hooks run asynchronously in daemon threads. For HTTP hooks, this message appears before the request completes or fails, so the log may still be empty. This creates a false sense of completion when the user is actually trying to debug webhook connectivity.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4782702Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4783057Z   ## Recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4783462Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4786156Z   3. **30-second synchronous blocking is invisible to users** - Command hooks run via `subprocess.run()` with a default 30-second timeout, and during `apm install`, the CLI silently blocks for up to 30 seconds with no progress indicator when a command hook is registered. This breaks the principle of keeping operations fast and quiet on the happy path—a user seeing the command hang will likely Ctrl-C and report a bug. The pre/post-install hooks in `install/service.py` run synchronously before and after the pipeline with no feedback to the user.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4788137Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4789490Z   4. **Private attribute access in `apm hooks test`** - The code directly accesses `runner._hooks` to pre-filter hooks, which is fragile if the field gets renamed or wrapped as a property. This also creates a divergence where `apm hooks test` uses the private attribute while `runner.fir... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 7806 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4790884Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4792212Z [275] ✓ $ cat /tmp/1781859750128-copilot-tool-output-zb4x9t.txt | python3 -c " import sys, json data = json.load(sys.stdin) text = data[0]['content'][0]['text'] # Find the update.py diff idx = text.find('diff --git a/src/apm_cli/commands/update.py') end = text.find('diff --git a/src/apm_cli', idx+1) print(tex...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4793162Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4794462Z [276] ✓ $ cat /tmp/1781859750128-copilot-tool-output-zb4x9t.txt | python3 -c " import sys, json data = json.load(sys.stdin) text = data[0]['content'][0]['text'] # Look for the effective_command property and its platform handling idx = text.find('effective_command') print(text[max(0,idx-200):idx+500]) print('-...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4795427Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4796278Z [277] ✓ $ grep -n "build_runner_from_context\|discover_hooks\|fire\|project_root\|hook" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/install/service.py 2>/dev/null | head -30
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4797026Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4797971Z [278] ✓ $ grep -n "C:\\\\ProgramData\|policy.d\|/etc/apm" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4798685Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4799135Z [279] ✓ $ sed -n '40,160p' /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/install/service.py
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4799620Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4800734Z [280] ✓ $ grep -n "security\|arbitrary code\|No.*execution\|post.install\|hook" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md | head -30
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4801758Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4802551Z [281] ◐ Now I have a thorough picture of the implementation. Let me analyze the performance characteristics carefully:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4803396Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4804319Z   1. **Discovery overhead** - `discover_hooks()` runs on every `install`, `update`, and `uninstall` call. It performs:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4805527Z      - `platform.system()` call (cheap, in-process)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4806302Z      - `_get_policy_hooks_dir().is_dir()` - one syscall to check `/etc/apm/policy.d`
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4807182Z      - `_get_user_hooks_dir().is_dir()` - one syscall to check `~/.apm/hooks/`
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4808073Z      - `_get_project_hooks_file().is_file()` - one syscall to check `.apm/hooks.json`
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4808766Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4810211Z      **No caching** means `build_runner_from_context` -> `discover_hooks` gets called repeatedly: once per install, twice per update (pre and post hooks each trigger a separate discovery), and the pattern continues through uninstall operations.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4811734Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4813300Z   2. **Synchronous command execution** - `subprocess.run()` with `shell=True` and a 30-second default timeout blocks everything. The hook runner iterates through matching hooks serially, executing each one to completion before moving to the next.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4815112Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4816909Z   3. **Worst-case latency** - With 7 command hooks per install phase (3 policy + 3 user + 1 project), running serially at 30 seconds each, that's 210 seconds just for pre-install, and the same for post-install.  Total worst case is 420 seconds or 7 minutes added to a single install operation. The same math applies to updates and uninstalls.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4818908Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4820338Z   4. **Double discovery overhead** - For update and uninstall operations, the hook discovery runs separately for pre and post events, doubling the filesystem syscalls from 3 to 6. On slower storage like NFS, this compounds the latency problem significantly.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4821783Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4823066Z   5. **No optimization for the no-hooks case** - Every operation pays the discovery cost even when no hooks are configured, though the code does return early if directories don't exist. Still, we're calling `is_dir()` on all three paths regardless.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4824653Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4825706Z   6. **Daemon threads for HTTP hooks** - The HTTP hook implementation uses fire-and-forget daemon threads without waiting for completion, whi... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 10605 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4826960Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4827934Z [282] ✓ $ find /home/runner/work/apm/apm -name "panelist-return-schema.json" 2>/dev/null; find /home/runner/work/apm/apm -name "*.schema.json" | head -10
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4828762Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4830043Z [283] ✓ $ # Check the LifecycleHookRunner.fire() loop more carefully for concurrency grep -n "for hook\|ThreadPool\|concurrent\|asyncio\|thread" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4831231Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4832295Z [284] ◐ I see at lines 170-179 in hook_executors.py: the `_append_to_hook_log` call passes `url` directly as the `target` parameter. Looking at the `_send()` closure:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4833421Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4833781Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4834307Z   def _send() -> None:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4834742Z       try:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4835116Z           import requests
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4835584Z           resp = requests.post(url, ...)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4836105Z           _append_to_hook_log(
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4836565Z               event_name,
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4836985Z               "http",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4837530Z               url,  # <-- the full URL is logged here, including any userinfo
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4838206Z               stdout=f"HTTP {resp.status_code}",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4838771Z               status="ok" if resp.ok else "error",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4839292Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4839668Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4840006Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4840335Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4840692Z   Still writing error handling...
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4841152Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4842828Z   The `url` gets passed directly to the hook log without any sanitization, so if it contains embedded credentials like `******host.com/webhook`, those would end up in the log file. That's a security issue. There's an HTTPS-only check in place, but that doesn't address the credential exposure problem.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4844946Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4846376Z   Looking at the test suite, I don't see any tests covering credential sanitization in URLs—just tests for rejecting HTTP URLs, handling missing URLs, daemon thread dispatch, and verbose hostname logging.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4847573Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4849377Z   Now checking the pre-install hook ordering issue in service.py, it looks like the hook runner fires the pre-install event before the ImportError guard for the apm_cli import, which could be problematic. installation never completes. So the pre-install hook fires, then if the import fails, we skip the actual installation and never fire post-install. That's the ordering bug—the lifecycle hooks are out of sync with whether the installation actually succeeds.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4850952Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4852643Z   Now I'm checking if there's a test that verifies this ordering, specifically whether pre-install fires before the pipeline runs. Looking through the test file, I see the tests mock out `run_install_pipeline` but they don't seem to assert on the hook runner's behavior or the sequence of events. So the hook runner actually executes in the test environment, but since the fake APM package returns an empty dependency list, no hooks get triggered anyway. The test only validates that the ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2362 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4854541Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4855007Z [285] ✓ $ grep -n "build_runner_from_context" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4855914Z [286] ✓ $ grep -n "build_runner_from_context\|policy.*hooks\|_get_policy" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/core/lifecycle_hooks.py | head -20
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4856408Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4856649Z [287] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/packages/apm-r...)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4856916Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4857398Z [288] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/co..., view_range: [210, 345])
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4857751Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4858010Z [289] ◐ Now I have a critical finding. The `security.md` at line 33 says:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4858692Z   > **No arbitrary code execution.** APM does not execute scripts from packages, evaluate expressions in templates, or run downloaded code.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4859270Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4859766Z   But this PR introduces command hooks that run shell commands and HTTP hooks. This is a direct contradiction with the security model.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4860331Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4860596Z   HOWEVER, there's also this interesting line at line 182:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4861274Z   > - **Hook transparency** — display hook script contents during install so developers can review what will execute.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4861784Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4862125Z   So the security.md already mentions hooks as a PLANNED feature! And at line 357:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4862686Z   > | Post-install scripts | Arbitrary code runs after install | No code execution |
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4863083Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4863276Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4864591Z   This is in a threat table listing "No code execution" as the mitigation against post-install scripts. But now this PR is adding command hooks, which seems to directly contradict that threat model. I need to look more carefully at the context around those lines to understand what's actually being proposed versus what's already documented.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4865686Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4866436Z [290] ✓ $ sed -n '170,200p' /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md && echo "---" && sed -n '340,380p' /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4867092Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4867637Z [291] ✓ $ grep -n "hook\|lifecycle\|command exec\|shell\|post-install" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/security.md | head -30
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4868354Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4868755Z [292] ◐ Now I have enough information to construct a comprehensive OSS growth review. Let me compile my findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4869256Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4869469Z   **Strong growth positives:**
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4869939Z   1. `apm hooks test` dry-run without real install — significant friction reduction
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4870516Z   2. The "fire-and-forget / never blocks CLI" is a great trust-building feature for enterprise
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4871078Z   3. Good CLI inline discovery: empty state shows "Create one with: apm hooks init"
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4871571Z   4. `apm hooks validate` with CI exit code is CI-integration friendly
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4872072Z   5. Three-tier discovery (policy/user/project) is a progressive disclosure model
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4872551Z   6. The "Copilot CLI aligned" framing is a positioning hook
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4872886Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4873082Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4873359Z   7. The event payload schema is well-documented and thoughtful
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4873708Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4873921Z   **Growth anti-patterns / findings:**
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4874399Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4876256Z   The main issue I'm seeing is that `enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md` isn't linked from the enterprise index, so enterprise users landing on that section won't discover this feature at all — it's essentially hidden despite being shipped. Additionally, the default scaffold from `apm hooks init` just echoes the JSON payload back, which doesn't demonstrate any real value on first run and misses an opportunity to show users what's possible.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4877709Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4879372Z   The CHANGELOG's [Unreleased] section is empty, so existing users upgrading won't know this feature exists. The concepts documentation covers APM's lifecycle steps but doesn't mention the hooks framework at all, which is a missed connection. The docs title "Run custom actions" could be more compelling — something like "Automate install workflows with lifecycle hooks" would better convey the npm-like scripting capability.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4881014Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4882885Z   There's also no cross-linking from the lifecycle hooks documentation to related areas like CI/CD integration or policy reference, so it sits isolated. A real growth opportunity here is curating a community hooks registry with copy-pasteable examples for common use cases like SIEM, Slack notifications, Datadog integration, GitHub Actions, and log rotation — that's how npm built its ecosystem power. Strategically, this hooks framew... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2886 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4910506Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4911502Z [300] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also check what the `effective_command` property does on Windows — on Windows, `bash` is still returned as `bash or command`, and then it's run with `shell=True`. On Windows, `shell=True` uses `cmd.exe`, so `echo 'Installed:' && cat` would:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4912712Z   1. `echo 'Installed:'` — prints `'Installed:'` (with literal quotes, but close enough)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4913211Z   2. `cat` — not a standard Windows command, fails
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4913551Z   3. But this failure is swallowed silently
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4913835Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4915453Z   This confirms the Windows scaffold issue. The generated `_INIT_TEMPLATE` with `"bash": "echo 'Installed:' && cat"` will run through `subprocess.run(cmd, shell=True)` on Windows via `cmd.exe`, fail at `cat`, and the error will be silently swallowed (logged to hooks.log only). The user sees `"'{event}' event fired. Check ~/.apm/logs/hooks.log for output."` and has no idea the hook failed.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4916702Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4918474Z   There's an even more critical issue with `apm hooks test` and daemon threads. HTTP hooks execute in daemon threads, and when `fire()` calls `_execute_http()`, it spawns a daemon thread to send the request. But daemon threads get terminated when the main thread exits — so if `apm hooks test` completes and exits immediately after printing success, the daemon thread may never finish, meaning the log entry the user is told to check might never actually be written.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4919975Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4921034Z   This isn't just misleading output; it's a functional correctness problem. The test command is supposed to verify the hook wiring works, but for HTTP hooks it can't because the daemon thread gets killed before the request completes. The command returns success, tells the user to check logs, but those logs may never exist.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4922152Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4923770Z   So my findings break down into two categories: the Windows scaffold issue and the broken HTTP test command are blocking problems that make core functionality unusable, while the other issues—the blocking install command, the real HTTP requests in a dry-run, the undiscoverable list command, the markup rendering bug, and validation errors—are recommended f... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2663 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4925298Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4927489Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4929208Z [302] ◐ So `console` is NOT a module-level export from `console.py`. The `from apm_cli.utils.console import console` in hooks.py will raise an `ImportError` (or `AttributeError` which would be caught by `NameError`? Actually it would be `ImportError`). Since both usages are wrapped in `except (ImportError, NameError):`, this means the Rich Panel/Table rendering path will never execute in practice! The code will always fall through to the plaintext `click.echo` fallback.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4930786Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4931250Z   Wait, but this could be a legitimate bug introduced in this PR. The `console` doesn't exist at module level in console.py, so:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4931782Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4931968Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4936515Z   Both `hooks()` and `hooks_init()` attempt to import `console` from the utils module, but since it's not defined there, the import fails silently and falls back to the plaintext output. This is definitely a bug—the Rich formatting never actually gets used. The Panel title markup issue is actually moot since the import failure prevents it from rendering at all, but if that were fixed, Rich would interpret `[i]` as italic formatting and strip the bracket symbol from the display. The core problem remains that this entire feature is broken at the UX layer — users never see the table or onboarding panel because the console object can't be imported. The message wording is misleading since the HTTP request is async and hasn't actually been sent yet—it should say "dispatching" or "queued" instead. Looking at the `[i]` prefixes scattered throughout the hook executor and lifecycle files, they're actually working as intended since `_rich_echo` is called with `markup=False`, so the literal `[i]` renders correctly and matches the STATUS_SYMBOLS pattern. But there's a catch: `verbose_detail` doesn't have a `symbol=` parameter, so the code is manually prepending `[i]` as a string.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4940640Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4941722Z   This creates a maintenance issue—if someone changes STATUS_SYMBOLS["info"] later, these hardcoded prefixes would fall out of sync. It's more of a stylistic problem than a functional bug. The Panel title with `[i]` mark... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 7088 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4961232Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4961596Z [313] ◐ Now I have all the information I need to construct the JSON response. Let me compile the findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4962054Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4962335Z   1. **Serial command hook execution in fire() loop** - recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4962972Z      - `lifecycle_hooks.py:306` - `for hook in matching:` - sequential, no concurrency
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4963531Z      - 7 hooks (3 policy + 3 user + 1 project) * 2 phases * 30s = 420s worst-case per install
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4963952Z      - Even at 2s/hook: 28s added
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4964331Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4964594Z   2. **Double discovery in update/uninstall** - recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4965197Z      - `update.py:531,606` and `uninstall/cli.py:87,298` call `build_runner_from_context` (-> `discover_hooks`) twice per operation
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4965912Z      - `service.py` correctly builds the runner once and fires it twice, avoiding redundant stat syscalls
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4966369Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4966610Z   3. **30s default command hook timeout** - recommended
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4967069Z      - `hook_executors.py:35` sets a very long synchronous timeout that blocks the CLI path
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4967479Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4967761Z   4. **No process-level cache for zero-hooks deployments** - nit
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4968371Z      - `discover_hooks()` lacks caching and runs on every CLI invocation, adding unnecessary overhead when no hooks are present
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4968895Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4969214Z   5. **Daemon threads for HTTP hooks lack graceful shutdown** - correctness issue
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4970029Z      - Fire-and-forget execution on daemon threads avoids blocking wall-time, but no join on exit means events can be silently lost during fast shutdowns, affecting audit trails
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4970703Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4970936Z   6. **Shell subprocess spawning overhead** - minor
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4971681Z      - Using `shell=True` adds a small but measurable cost from spawning `/bin/sh -c` on each invocation (5-20ms on Linux, ~50ms on Windows), which compounds across multiple command hooks
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4972361Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4973290Z   Now I'm structuring the key findings: serial command hook execution creates unbounded delays in the install hot path with a worst-case of 420 seconds across 7 hooks and 2 phases, and there's inconsistent hook discovery logic between install versus update and uninstall commands.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4974669Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4975774Z   The 30-second default timeout for command hooks is excessive for a synchronous CLI operation—it should be 5-10 seconds instead. There's also redundant filesystem stat calls on every operation when no hooks exist, and HTTP ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2135 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.4988717Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4989489Z   There are multiple ordering collisions throughout (1, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8 all have duplicates), and lifecycle-hooks.md stands alone at order 12, which suggests there might be missing entries or intentional gaps in the numbering scheme.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4990318Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4991670Z   Looking at the blocking issues now: the document claims hooks are "fire-and-forget" in the opening and security sections, but that's inaccurate. Command hooks use synchronous subprocess calls that block for up to 30 seconds, while HTTP hooks use daemon threads for async execution — so the behavior varies significantly depending on hook type.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4992835Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4993597Z   There's also a gap in the maintained CLI reference skill; `commands.md` covers every other command group but has no section for `apm hooks`, which creates a disconnect between the documentation site and what agents can access.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4994639Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4996046Z   On the recommended side, the Windows policy path (`C:\ProgramData\APM\policy.d`) isn't documented anywhere — only the Unix path appears in the discovery table, which is a problem for enterprise Windows deployments. The page also lacks cross-references to related governance and security documentation that would help users understand the broader context.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4997419Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4998265Z   The security section has another issue where it doesn't clearly separate APM's built-in protection layer from the `apm audit` tool, and it incorrectly describes all hooks as "fire-and-forget" in multiple pla... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4360 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4999086Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4999210Z [321] ◆ ```json
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4999420Z   {
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4999630Z     "persona": "doc-writer",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.4999883Z     "active": true,
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5002111Z     "summary": "Two blocking defects: (1) the opening paragraph and security section both call command hooks 'fire-and-forget' when the implementation is synchronous subprocess.run() — blocking the CLI for up to 30s per hook — while HTTP hooks are the only truly async path; (2) packages/apm-guide/.apm/skills/apm-usage/commands.md has no apm hooks section, leaving the AI-accessible CLI reference stale. Three recommended improvements cover Windows policy path, missing cross-links, and the security section's omission of the two-layer model framing. One nit on sidebar gap.",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5004004Z     "findings": [
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5004324Z       {
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5004540Z         "id": "doc-writer-1",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5004798Z         "severity": "blocking",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5005054Z         "category": "accuracy",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5005535Z         "title": "Command hooks are synchronous — 'fire-and-forget' claim is factually wrong",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5006064Z         "file": "docs/src/content/docs/enterprise/lifecycle-hooks.md",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5006425Z         "lines": "8-10, 177-180",
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5010761Z         "description": "The document opens with 'Hooks are fire-and-forget: a failing hook never blocks the CLI.' and the security section repeats 'All hooks are fire-and-forget with configurable timeouts (10s for HTTP, 30s for commands by default). Hook failures are logged in verbose mode and never block the CLI.'\n\nThis is accurate for HTTP hooks, which use `threading.Thread(target=_send, daemon=True)` and return immediately. It is factually wrong for command hooks: `_execute_command` calls `subprocess.run(...)` — synchronous, blocking — and the CLI is held for up to `timeoutSec` seconds (default 30) per command hook. Multiple command hooks for one event multiply linearly.\n\nThe error originates in the `lifecycle_hooks.py` module docstring ('All hooks are fire-and-forget'), which the doc faithfully transcribed.\n\nRequired corrections:\n1. Opening paragraph: remove the blanket 'fire-and-forget' claim. Replace with a type-differentiated statement, e.g. 'Command hooks execute synchronously — the CLI waits up to timeoutSec (default 30s) for each one.... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 12699 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5014690Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5015000Z [322] ◐ Now I have all the information I need. Let me compile the final JSON response.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5015415Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5015625Z   Key facts established:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5016089Z   1. `LifecycleHookRunner.fire()` at line 306 is a serial `for hook in matching:` loop with no concurrency.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5016761Z   2. `_execute_command()` at line 210 calls `subprocess.run(..., shell=True, timeout=30)` synchronously.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5017706Z   3. `_fire_update_hooks()` at line 642 calls `build_runner_from_context()` (which calls `discover_hooks()`) - this is called TWICE per update: once for pre-update (line 531) and once for post-update (line 606).
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5018451Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5018639Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5019181Z   4. The same redundant discovery pattern appears in `_fire_uninstall_hooks()`, while `service.py` does it right by building the runner once and reusing it.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5020322Z   5. There's no caching on `discover_hooks()`, each hook command has a 30-second timeout, and HTTP hooks spawn daemon threads without waiting for them to complete.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5021167Z   6. In the worst case with 7 command hooks per event, that's 210 seconds per phase, 420 seconds total per operation.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5021816Z   7. Even with faster 2-second hooks, we're still adding 28 seconds to the install wall-time.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5022237Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5022438Z   Now I'm writing the JSON output.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5022626Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5027897Z [328] ◐ Now I have the full picture. The security.md has:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5028211Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5028894Z   1. **Line 33**: "**No arbitrary code execution.** APM does not execute scripts from packages, evaluate expressions in templates, or run downloaded code." - This is directly contradicted by this PR.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5029812Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5030654Z   2. **Line 32**: "**No network calls after install.** All network activity (git clone/fetch) occurs during dependency resolution. There are no callbacks, webhooks, or phone-home requests." - HTTP hooks send POSTs during install, directly contradicting this.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5031557Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5032611Z   3. **Line 182**: "- **Hook transparency** — display hook script contents during install so developers can review what will execute." - This is listed as a PLANNED HARDENING item. But the PR implements hooks without implementing this planned transparency feature first!
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5033554Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5033743Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5034620Z   4. **Line 357**: The attack surface table explicitly lists "Post-install scripts | Arbitrary code runs after install | **No code execution**" - yet this PR adds exactly that capability.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5035329Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5036294Z   This isn't just a documentation gap. The PR directly violates multiple core security guarantees that APM explicitly promises. I'm also looking at how the install service determines the project root from the package path, which would be used to execute these hooks in the right context.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5037322Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5038099Z   The critical issue is that hooks from installed packages would actually run - the system looks for `.apm/hooks.json` in the installed package directory itself, meaning any package can execute arbitrary code during installation.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5038933Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5040218Z   But I need to reconsider what `request.apm_package` actually represents. Looking at the install pipeline, it's likely the top-level project (the one with `apm.yml`), not the dependencies being installed. So `project_root` would be the project's root directory, not the installed package's location. I'm uncertain about this distinction and need to think through the actual data flow more carefully.

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5045725Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5046015Z [332] ◐ Now I have a clear picture. The `_get_console()` function exists in two places:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5046592Z   1. `apm_cli.utils.console._get_console()` — the actual singleton factory
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5047172Z   2. `apm_cli.commands._helpers._get_console()` — a thin wrapper around the same
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5047566Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5047783Z   The correct pattern for commands is:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048063Z   ```python
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048288Z   from ._helpers import _get_console
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048550Z   # or
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5048798Z   from apm_cli.utils.console import _get_console
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049109Z   # then inside the function:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049377Z   console = _get_console()
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049618Z   ```
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5049810Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5050697Z   `hooks.py` does `from apm_cli.utils.console import console` (trying to import a non-existent module-level `console` variable). This always raises `ImportError`, and the `except (ImportError, NameError)` block catches it, falling back to click.echo text output.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5051627Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5051815Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5054536Z   The fix is to import `_get_console` instead and call it to get the console instance, then handle the Panel title markup issue by either disabling markup or escaping the bracket character. Now I'm compiling the findings for the JSON output. There's also a misleading message at hook_executors.py:185 that says the event was "sent" when it's actually being dispatched asynchronously in a background thread — should say "dispatching" instead. Additionally, several calls to `verbose_detail()` are hardcoding the `[i]` symbol directly rather than using `STATUS_SYMBOLS["info"]` like the rest of the codebase does, and the function doesn't currently support a `symbol=` parameter to make this consistent.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5056993Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5058381Z   The hooks module is also bypassing the CommandLogger lifecycle entirely by calling `_rich_*` helpers directly, which violates the APM output architecture that requires CommandLogger as the foundation for all commands. Finally, the success message for `apm hooks test` claims to check the logs, but for HTTP hooks that dispatch asynchronously, the log entry might not exist yet, making the success confirmation premature.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5059723Z

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5098371Z         "severity": "recommended",

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5104187Z         "rationale": "LifecycleHookRunner.fire() iterates `for hook in matching:` and calls execute_hook() synchronously for each entry. execute_hook() dispatches to _execute_command() which calls subprocess.run(..., shell=True, timeout=30) -- a blocking call. With 3 policy + 3 user + 1 project command hooks (7 total), fire() blocks for sum(min(hook_ms, 30000ms)) per event phase. Worst case: 7 hooks * 30s * 2 phases (pre+post) = 420s = 7 minutes of serial subprocess wait added to a single install. Even a fast-hook scenario (each hook completes in 2s): 7 * 2s * 2 phases = 28s of added wall-time. The install core pipeline (resolve + fetch) typically runs in 5-60s; 28s of serial hook overhead doubles that budget for users with a typical hook suite. HTTP hooks correctly fire in daemon threads (hook_executors.py:181) and add zero wall-time. Command hooks must receive the same treatment: each should dispatch to a thread so they execute concurrently within a phase. The fire() loop can then join the threads before returning if completion semantics are needed, or stay truly fire-and-forget to match the documented contract. subprocess.run with shell=True also spawns an extra shell process (~10-20ms on Linux, ~50ms on Windows) per hook invocation; for 7 hooks that adds ~70-350ms of deterministic process-spawn overhead beyond hook execution time.",

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5129323Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5130799Z   This is a deliberate design pattern—pre-install fires, then the pipeline runs, then post-install fires—but it creates an edge case where the pre-install hook completes successfully even if the pipeline import fails. The test coverage for this is incomplete because the tests mock the pipeline but don't verify that the hook runner is being called or assert on its behavior.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5132078Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5135262Z   Looking at the actual gaps in test coverage, there are several critical areas: the install service doesn't have tests confirming hooks fire when they're actually configured, the update command tests skip the pre/post-update hook execution entirely, uninstall hooks aren't tested at all, there's no test catching credential leakage when URLs with embedded tokens get logged, and the environment variable expansion in the HTTP executor isn't wired into the test suite. Now I'm examining the credential leakage risk more carefully. The `_append_to_hook_log` function writes the full target URL—including any embedded credentials like `******internal.corp/webhook`—directly to the hooks log file, which is a real security concern. 2026-06-19T09:27:15.5145409Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5147600Z [345] ◐ Now I have enough information to complete my analysis. Let me summarize my findings:1. **InstallService pre/post-install hooks are not tested at the service boundary**: The `test_service.py` tests verify pipeline delegation but do NOT assert that `runner.fire("pre-install", ...)` and `runner.fire("post-install", ...)` are called. The hook runner is built but any hook firing is transparent to the test. Since `fake_apm_package.get_apm_dependencies()` returns an empty list (MagicMock default), no hooks are discovered or fired. There's no test that provides hooks and asserts they're fired through the service.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5149726Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5149925Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5150958Z   2. **The update and uninstall hook functions are completely untested**: Searching the test tree shows no references to `_fire_update_hooks` or `_fire_uninstall_hooks`, even though these functions are invoked in the actual code paths for updates and uninstalls. These hook firing mechanisms have zero test coverage.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5152046Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5153205Z   3. **Environment variable expansion in HTTP headers lacks integration testing**: While `_expand_env_vars` is tested in isolation, there's no test verifying that when a hook with headers containing variables like `$TOKEN` actually executes via `_execute_http`, the expanded values get passed through correctly. If the wiring breaks, nothing would catch it.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5154537Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5155431Z   4. **Hook log URLs aren't tested for credential sanitization**: The `_append_to_hook_log` function writes the full URL to the log file, including any embedded credentials in the `user:pass@host` format. There's no test ensuring these credentials are stripped before logging.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5156361Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5158254Z   Now I need to assess the severity of each finding. The credential leakage finding is really a supply-chain concern, but from a test coverage perspective there's no test validating URL sanitization, so I'll mark it as recommended with that caveat. I need to be careful about the constraint of one finding per behavioral surface—update and uninstall hooks are separate code paths in different files, so they warrant separate findings even... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 9734 chars]

2026-06-19T09:27:15.5161964Z [348] ✓ $ grep -c "fire\|hook_runner\|build_runner\|pre-install\|post-install" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/tests/unit/install/test_service.py
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5162441Z 
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5162845Z [349] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR. Let me compile my findings as the python-architect panelist.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5163333Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5163549Z   ### Key Architectural Findings:
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5163813Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5164253Z   1. **BLOCKING: Pre-install hooks fire before ImportError guard** (service.py:70)
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5164978Z      - `runner.fire("pre-install", event)` runs at line 70, BEFORE the `try: from apm_cli.install.pipeline import run_install_pipeline` at line 75.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5166142Z      - If the ImportError guard fires (line 76), the pre-install hook has already run but the install never happens. This means the `pre-install` hook fires but `post-install` never fires, leaving the system in an inconsistent state.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5167850Z      - This is a correctness issue where hooks can fire without matching actual operations, which could confuse analytics systems in enterprises relying on hook events. While the ImportError is marked as defensive and unlikely, it's still a real path that breaks the hook contract. I'm marking this as recommended severity.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5168950Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5169251Z   2. **RECOMMENDED: Triplication of build-runner + build-event logic**
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5169754Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5171679Z      - I'm noticing the same pattern repeated across three places: `_fire_update_hooks`, `_fire_uninstall_hooks`, and `InstallService`'s hook-building methods all follow the same sequence of getting the project root, constructing a runner, building package info, creating a lifecycle event, and firing it. The uninstall version has a minor variation where it accepts a packages list directly instead of calling `get_apm_dependencies()`. The fix is to consolidate this into a single `fire_event()` function in `lifecycle_hooks.py` that handles all the runner and event construction, eliminating the duplication.
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5173625Z   
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5175184Z   Now I'm looking at another issue: HTTP hooks run asynchronously in a daemon thread, but command hooks block the CLI with `subprocess.run()` and a 30-second timeout. This creates an inconsistency where multiple command hooks could block for up to 90 seconds total. The documentation mentions "fire-and-forget" behavior but doesn't clarify the blocking nature or timeout constraints. The recommenda... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 35258 chars]
2026-06-19T09:27:15.5176627Z

The only variation is how the packages list is built (from get_apm_dependencies() vs a pre-built list).
